content.problemStatement:
You are given a matrix of integers AAA with NNN rows (numbered 111 through NNN) and MMM columns (numbered 111 through MMM). Each element of this matrix is either 000 or 111.

A move consists of the following steps:


Choose two different rows r1r_1r1​ and r2r_2r2​ or two different columns c1c_1c1​ and c2c_2c2​.
Apply the bitwise OR operation with the second row/column on the first row/column. Formally, if you chose two rows, this means you should change Ar1,kA_{r_1, k}Ar1​,k​ to Ar1,k∨Ar2,kA_{r_1, k} \lor A_{r_2, k}Ar1​,k​∨Ar2​,k​ for each 1≤k≤M1 \le k \le M1≤k≤M; if you chose two columns, then you should change Ak,c1A_{k, c_1}Ak,c1​​ to Ak,c1∨Ak,c2A_{k, c_1} \lor A_{k, c_2}Ak,c1​​∨Ak,c2​​ for each 1≤k≤N1 \le k \le N1≤k≤N.
For each element of the matrix, compute the minimum number of moves required to make it equal to 111 or determine that it is impossible. Note that these answers are independent, i.e. we are starting with the initial matrix for each of them.
constraints: A_{i, j} ∈ {0, 1} for each valid i, j
The sum of N * M for all test cases does not exceed 1,000,000
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N and M. N lines follow. For each i (1 ≤ i ≤ N), the i-th of these lines contains M integers A_{i, 1}, A_{i, 2}, …, A_{i, M}.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print N lines. For each valid i, the i-th of these lines should contain M space-separated integers; for each valid j, the j-th of these integers should be the minimum number of moves required to make A_{i, j} equal to 1, or -1 if it is impossible.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the minimum number of moves required to make each element of the matrix equal to 1 is as follows: A[0][0] = 1 (no moves), A[0][1] = 0 (1 move), A[0][2] = 1 (no moves), A[1][0] = 2 (2 moves), A[1][1] = 1 (1 move), A[1][2] = 1 (1 move), A[2][0] = 1 (1 move), A[2][1] = 1 (1 move), A[2][2] = 0 (no moves).

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use dynamic programming to keep track of the minimum number of moves required to make each element of the matrix equal to 1. We start by initializing a 2D array dp of size N x M with all elements set to -1. Then, we iterate over each element of the matrix. If the current element is 1, we set dp[i][j] to 0 because no moves are required to make it 1. If the current element is 0, we check if there is a row or column that contains a 1. If we find such a row or column, we update dp[i][j] with the minimum number of moves required to make the current element 1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * M) — because we are iterating over each test case, and for each test case, we are iterating over each element of the matrix.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * M) — because we are using a 2D array dp of size N x M to store the minimum number of moves required to make each element of the matrix equal to 1.
